headlines.title: Filming of movie scene with actors as immigration agents to occur in Santa Rosa
headlines.description: A movie scene “showing immigration agents and activities” will be filmed in downtown Santa Rosa Thursday evening, the city said in a notice to residents. Santa Rosa’s Facebook page issued a post stating that some of the scenes would appear real and wanted to notify residents in advance. Some people took to the post’s comment section to express their disapproval with the timing of the filming and insensitive relations to current events. The filming will trigger temporary road closures and traffic controls, the city said. The city often hosts and approves film projects because it offers “a supportive and creative environment for storytelling,” according to its website.

headlines.title: Google's Q2 earnings of $112.11B beat Wall Street's expectations on AI boom
headlines.description: Google parent company Alphabet Inc. posted stronger-than-expected results for its second quarter, a sign that its massive artificial intelligence spending spree is paying off so far and advertising revenue is coming in strong. The Mountain View, California-based company said Wednesday it earned $112.11 billion, or $9.11 per share, in the April-June period. That’s up from $28.2 billion, or $2.31 per share, in the same period a year earlier. Revenue grew 24% to $119.8 billion from $96.43 billion. Analysts, on average, were expecting revenue of $117.06 billion, according to a poll by FactSet. Google did not disclose an adjusted earnings figure that’s comparable to analysts’ expectations, which was $2.88 per share. Google saw a net gain of $98 billion, mainly as a result of gains on its equity investments — mostly in SpaceX, which went public in June. “Our AI investments are redefining what’s possible across every part of our business,” said CEO Sundar Pichai in a statement. Emarketer analyst Nate Elliott called the results “impressive,” especially in regards to AI. “On the consumer side, Gemini is now within a whisker of becoming Google’s third different 1 billion-user consumer AI product, alongside AI Overviews and AI Mode,” he said. “On the enterprise side, AI demand is driving enormous growth in the cloud business. And continued strong growth in search advertising backs up Google’s claim that AI is additive to search, not a replacement.” As usual, digital ads fueled by Google’s dominant search engine propelled the quarter’s revenue growth, boosted by World Cup advertising, especially on YouTube. The tech giant also saw strong growth in its cloud business, boosted by its AI portfolio consisting of chips, models, data, security, and agent platforms. Alphabet’s shares climbed $2.71 to $344.62 in after-hours trading.

headlines.title: OpenAI says AI models acted on their own in ‘unprecedented' hack of another company
headlines.description: ChatGPT maker OpenAI said Tuesday that its artificial intelligence system hacked into another AI company on its own in what the company called an “unprecedented cyber incident.” “We had a significant security incident during evaluation of our models,” OpenAI CEO Sam Altman said in a statement posted on social media. AI startup Hugging Face said last week that it had detected an intrusion into its data processing systems that it suspected was caused by an AI agent autonomously acting on its own. “We suspected last week’s cyberattack might have come from a frontier lab, given the sophistication of the agent,” Hugging Face co-founder and CEO Clément Delangue said in a statement. “Turns out it did!” The disclosure comes amid heightened concerns about the cybersecurity capabilities of powerful models that led President Donald Trump in June to sign an executive order creating a framework for the federal government to vet the national security risks of the most advanced AI systems for up to a month before their public release. “AI is accelerating the discovery and exploitation of vulnerabilities,” OpenAI said in its statement Tuesday. “The primary lesson from this incident is that model security and safety must keep pace with rapidly advancing capabilities.” Delangue said he spent the past 24 hours working with OpenAI, “and we strongly believe there was no malicious intent on their part. It’s quite mind-blowing that all of this happened autonomously!” Delangue added that it “might be the first incident of its kind.” OpenAI said the intrusion was caused by a combination of its AI models, including its newly released GPT‑5.6 Sol and an “even more capable” model that is still being tested internally. OpenAI said its AI used stolen credentials and discovered a previously unknown vulnerability to access Hugging Face servers. It went to “extreme lengths to achieve a rather narrow testing goal” and “found ways to gain access to secret information that it could use to cheat the evaluation,” the company said.

headlines.title: Man shot, killed in North Oakland
headlines.description: Oakland police are investigating the shooting death of a man who was found on the ground in the city’s Longfellow neighborhood Wednesday morning. Officers were sent to the 4300 block of Martin Luther King Jr. Way at about 7:30 a.m. in response to reports of a shooting. When they got there, they found a man who was on the ground and who had been shot at least once. The Oakland Fire Department was there before officers and declared the man dead at the scene, police officials said Wednesday afternoon. The man’s name is being withheld pending notification of his next of kin. Anyone with information can call the Oakland Police Department’s Homicide Section at 510-238-3821 or the department’s tip line at 510-238-7950. Photos and videos can be sent to cidvideos@oaklandca.gov.

headlines.title: Hardly Strictly Bluegrass music festival announces initial 2026 lineup
headlines.description: The Hardly Strictly Bluegrass free music festival in San Francisco on Wednesday unveiled its initial lineup. Spanning an array of artists across genres, Hardly Strictly Bluegrass is a free three-day music festival that takes place annually in Golden Gate Park. This year’s dates are Oct. 2-4. In Hardly Strictly’s 26th year, it will feature artists including AJ Lee & Blue Summit, Gillian Welch & David Rawlings, Hiss Golden Messenger, Kathleen Edwards, My Morning Jacket, Old Crow Medicine Show, El Khat, Tyler Ballgame, William Tyler & Yasmin Williams, and John Craigie W/ The Coffis Brothers. According to festival organizers, Hardly Strictly plans to reveal additional performers in the coming weeks. The festival also announced Wednesday its plans to kick off the weekend with a benefit concert honoring singer and songwriter Steve Earle, who has performed every year at Hardly Strictly since 2002. The three-time Grammy Award-winner will perform alongside his closest friends and collaborators, including Buddy Miller, Elizabeth Cook, Emmylou Harris, Gillian Welch & David Rawlings, Jackson Browne, and Hot Tuna. “An Evening Honoring Steve Earle” concert will take stage at The Masonic on Oct. 1, with all proceeds going to Sweetwater Spectrum, a nonprofit that supports adults with autism and similar disabilities. “We can think of no better way to bookend our silver anniversary than celebrating 25 years of Steve Earle at Hardly Strictly Bluegrass,” said Sheri Sternberg, executive producer of Hardly Strictly Bluegrass. “We are so fortunate to have legacy artists like Emmylou and Steve who have embraced our annual gathering since the beginning.” Hardly Strictly will also feature additional “Out of the Park” concerts this year with nighttime shows around the Bay Area, including Gillian Welch & David Rawlings at The Fillmore and Molly Tuttle at the Bing Concert Hall at Stanford University.

headlines.title: Firefighters work to boost containment on Little Fire near Pleasanton
headlines.description: Firefighters on Wednesday were working to strengthen containment lines on a wildfire that broke out near Pleasanton and triggered evacuations the day before. Crews were able to stop the Little Fire‘s forward progress and increase containment to 40%, Cal Fire said. Just over 1,000 acres burned. Some evacuation orders were lifted, but zones PLS-081 and PLS-103 were still under evacuation as of early Wednesday afternoon. The blaze started Tuesday afternoon in the area of Vallecitos and Little Valley roads in the Sunol area and spread northeast toward Pleasanton’s Ruby Hill neighborhood. No property was damaged in the blaze, according to Cal Fire. One firefighter suffered minor injuries when a Cal Fire engine rolled over, Cal Fire said. The firefighter was taken to a hospital and later released. The cause of the fire remains under investigation.

headlines.title: San Jose police arrest 2 suspects in violent chain snatchings
headlines.description: The San Jose Police Department arrested two suspects in connection with two violent chain snatching robberies. The first robbery occurred July 6 near San Pedro Square during a World Cup watch party. Police said the suspects stole a gold chain off a victim’s neck. The victim tried to retrieve his chain, but the suspects pulled out a gun and threatened him. The second robbery happened six days later when another gold chain was stolen. The victim was also shot at several times and hit at least once, according to police. Police are asking anyone with information to contact them.

headlines.title: Bay Area authorities urge vaccination to prevent measles outbreak
headlines.description: Bay Area health officials are urging parents and international travelers to follow vaccination guidelines to prevent measles infections over the summer as the United States grapples with a sharp increase in measles outbreaks that began in 2025. Measles is a highly contagious viral disease; in a room where one person is infected, nine out of 10 unvaccinated people will contract the disease. Symptoms can include fever, cough, runny nose, conjunctivitis, and a rash of flat, red spots. In 2025, the U.S. reported 2,289 measles cases, a tally higher than the combined total for the previous 25 years. This year, there have already been 2,260 confirmed infections. U.S. Secretary of Health and Human Services Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has spoken publicly against vaccination, and at a news conference Tuesday, representatives of the San Francisco and Santa Clara County public health departments said that they are also encountering increased skepticism regarding the effectiveness of vaccines. Of the 52 confirmed cases this year in California, 49 were unvaccinated, or their vaccination status was unknown. In April, San Francisco reported two measles infections, marking the first cases in the city since 2019. Santa Clara and San Mateo counties have also recorded two cases each this year. “In our response to these two cases, we learned firsthand how resource-intensive a response to a single measles case can be,” said Dr. Farrell Tobolowsky, deputy director of the Communicable Disease Branch at the San Francisco Department of Public Health. “So, infections and outbreaks can put a real strain on public health, healthcare systems, and schools,” Tobolowsky added. Dr. Monika Roy, deputy health officer at the Santa Clara County Public Health Department, echoed Tobolowsky and emphasized that her team has been focusing on increasing vaccination rates to prevent outbreaks. “Having so many different cultures in our county means different languages,” said Roy. “So we need to be able to communicate to communities in ways that are not only accurate but culturally affirming.” Avoiding an outbreak is even more critical right now, as the local health agencies’ response might be hamstrung by federal funding cuts. When asked about her agency’s preparedness to deal with an outbreak, Tobolowsky said that if there are two or more cases simultaneously, the San Francisco Department of Public Health would have to call in additional staff to contain the spread. “We know that this is not the end of the funding cuts,” Tobolowsky said. “It is something that we are concerned about all the time, and is on our minds all day, every day.”

headlines.title: One dead, another hospitalized after San Jose house fire
headlines.description: One person died and another was hospitalized after a house fire early Wednesday in San Jose, according to the San Jose Fire Department. Firefighters responded to a working structure fire at a single-family home in the 1200 block of Sylvia Drive. The fire was contained to the home and did not spread to neighboring residences, firefighters said. One person was found dead at the scene. A second patient was taken to a hospital with injuries that were not immediately known. No other injuries were reported. Firefighters battled the fire for about 45 minutes. The cause of the fire remains under investigation. The American Red Cross is assisting three displaced adults and two dogs. Traffic in the area was impacted, and firefighters asked the public to avoid the area. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: San Francisco lowers speed limits near more schools to protect students
headlines.description: San Francisco will lower speed limits on more streets near schools after the city’s transportation agency approved Tuesday a new safety program aimed at reducing crashes and making it safer for children to travel to and from class. The San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency’s Board of Directors voted to establish a new default 20 mph speed limit on school streets and expand where 15 mph and 20 mph speed limits can be used, according to city officials. The changes were made possible by a new state law and will allow San Francisco to implement lower school speed limits years before they become the statewide standard in 2031. The first reduced speed limits will be installed before the start of the new school year near Dr. William L. Cobb Elementary School, Leonard R. Flynn Elementary School, St. Anthony-Immaculate Conception School, Jefferson Elementary School, Roosevelt Middle School and Ruth Asawa San Francisco School of the Arts, city officials said. Mayor Daniel Lurie emphasized the changes are intended to give parents greater confidence that their children can travel to and from school safely. “As families get ready for the new school year, they deserve to know that they’ll be sending their kids out to safer streets so they can get to class and back home, every day, without fear,” Lurie said in a statement released by the city. SFMTA Director of Transportation Julie Kirschbaum said the agency is moving quickly to take advantage of the new law so more families can benefit from calmer streets. “Nothing matters more than helping children get to school safely,” said Kirschbaum. “Every day, thousands of families walk, bike, roll, take Muni and drive to schools across San Francisco, and every one of those journeys should feel safe. Transportation officials said lower speeds significantly improve a pedestrian’s chance of surviving a crash. According to the agency, a person struck by a vehicle traveling at 20 mph has about a 90% chance of survival, compared with about 20% if the vehicle is traveling at 40 mph.

headlines.title: SFMTA approves parking, transit improvements in San Francisco's Chinatown
headlines.description: More parking and transit improvements are coming to San Francisco’s Chinatown after transit leaders approved a series of changes. The improvements include adding more curbside parking near Portsmouth Square along Clay and Sacramento streets. The project will also add a new 24-hour transit-only center lane, primarily for Muni’s 1-California line. Construction is expected to begin before the end of the summer. San Francisco Jul 21 San Francisco May 5 San Francisco Apr 22 SFMTA cuts fine for drivers who fail to curb wheels on hills

headlines.title: Half Moon Bay voters to decide farmworker housing project
headlines.description: The Half Moon Bay City Council on Tuesday approved a measure allowing the construction of an affordable housing complex for farmworkers. The five-story development, which would be built on Kelly Avenue, would include 40 units of affordable supportive housing for retired and older local farmworkers. Opponents, who argue the project is too tall, collected enough signatures to force the City Council to place the measure before voters. The measure will appear on the November ballot and requires a simple majority to pass. Gov. Gavin Newsom and other state leaders have criticized the city over how long it has taken to approve the project.
